# Chapter 3: The Vote

The hail came through professional, almost bored. "Freighter *Dusty Comet*, this is recovery vessel *Patience*. Cut engines and prepare for inspection. We're here to retrieve misrouted corporate property. This doesn't have to be complicated."

Reese muted the channel. "Kellen, what are we looking at?"

"Light frigate, corporate security configuration. Better guns, better engines, better everything." Kellen's hands moved across the console. "We can't outrun them. Not in a straight burn."

"They armed?" Tav asked.

"Retrieval teams don't usually shoot," Reese said. "Bad for the cargo."

"Usually?"

Reese didn't answer. She opened the channel. "This is *Comet*. We're carrying authorized freight with proper documentation. What property?"

"Container loaded at Station 47, six hours ago. We have transfer codes. Stand by for boarding."

Reese muted again. Her mind was running numbers: time to Hadrian Station, jump points, fuel reserves, corporate patrol patterns. All bad.

"We hand it over," Kellen said.

Tav turned. "What?"

"We hand over the crate, we get paid, we leave." Kellen wasn't looking at either of them, just at the engineering readouts. "Job's done."

"They're going to kill it."

"You don't know that."

"Kellen, you saw the setup. That's not—"

"I saw a sedated specimen in a transport crate with corporate paperwork." Kellen's voice was level. "Same as every other shady cargo job we've taken. We don't know what it is. We don't know what they're doing with it. We know we need the money."

Reese was still watching the sensor blip. Fifteen minutes out now.

"Captain," Tav said. "We can't just—"

"If we hand it over after opening the crate," Reese said slowly, "Reed might not pay the second half. Contract violation."

Kellen made a frustrated sound. "Fine. We argue for full payment, claim the seal broke accidentally. They get their specimen, we get our money. Everyone walks away."

"Except the thing in the crate," Tav said.

"The thing in the crate isn't our responsibility." Kellen turned to face them. "I have family on Redrock Station. My sister and her kid. I send them half my pay every month because station work pays shit and Davey needs medication that costs more than she makes. You want to play hero with my nephew's medicine?"

Tav's face went red. "That's not—I'm not—"

"Then what are you doing? We're cargo haulers. We haul cargo. Sometimes it's nice cargo, sometimes it's not. We don't get to pick."

"Yes we do," Tav said. "That's exactly what we get to pick."

"Easy to say when you don't have people depending on you."

The words hit and Tav flinched. Reese saw their hands curl into fists.

"You don't know what I—"

"Enough," Reese said.

They both stopped. The bridge was quiet except for the proximity alarm, counting down.

Reese looked at the sensor display. The corporate ship was changing course, angling to intercept. She thought about the crate in the cargo bay. The creature's eyes, silver and knowing. The shock collar. The restraints. The manifests that said specimen and threat assessment and quarantine wing.

She thought about Jericho-7. The holding cells. The screaming.

"If we run," Kellen said quietly, "we burn every contact in this sector. Reed's network, probably others. We can't dock anywhere corporate-affiliated. That's most stations."

"I know."

"We've got fuel for maybe one jump, two if we coast. No funds for resupply until Hadrian, which we won't make if we don't deliver."

"I know."

"So what's the play, Captain?"

Reese pulled up the star charts. Looked at their options. All of them bad.

She'd spent twenty years with Orion. Started in facility security—standing at doors, checking credentials, filing reports. Moved up to transport security. Then specimen retrieval. That's what they called it. Retrieval. Like the things they were taking weren't alive, weren't aware, weren't screaming in frequencies that made your teeth ache.

She'd told herself it was legal. Corporate property, proper authorization. Told herself for fifteen years.

Jericho-7, she'd stopped telling herself that.

And now here she was, six years later, with another crate and another specimen and another retrieval team.

"Did you read the whole manifest?" she asked Kellen.

"Most of it. Why?"

"Specimen's species origin?"

Kellen pulled up the datapad, scrolled. "Designation... Hadrian-9. Wait." They scrolled more. "Shit. Hadrian-9 was glassed in the war. Twenty years ago."

"Thought extinct," Reese said.

"They think this is the last one?"

Reese didn't answer. She was looking at Tav, who was watching her with desperate hope that made her want to throw them off the ship.

"You worked for them," Tav said. "For Orion. You know what they do."

"Yeah. I do."

"So—"

"So I know this choice is stupid. I know we can't win. I know Kellen's right about the money and the contacts and everything else." Reese's hand hovered over the controls. "I also know I didn't spend six years running indie jobs just to hand over cargo to the same bastards I left."

"Captain," Kellen said. Warning or question, Reese wasn't sure.

She looked at them. "I'm not asking you to agree. I'm telling you what I'm doing."

"Which is?"

"Kellen, how fast can we burn?"

Kellen stared at her. "This is stupid."

"How fast?"

"Redline? Hundred-ten percent. For maybe an hour before something melts."

"Do it."

"Reese—"

"That's an order." She opened the channel to the *Patience*. "This is *Comet*. We're declining your inspection. Have a nice day."

She cut the channel and fired engines.

The *Dusty Comet* shuddered, groaned, and surged forward. Behind them, the corporate ship adjusted course. Kellen swore steadily, hands flying across engineering controls, rerouting power, pushing the engines past safe limits.

Tav was gripping their console, looking between Reese and the sensor display. "Thank you," they said.

"Don't," Reese said. "Don't make this noble. I just hate those bastards more than I like money."

From the cargo bay below, filtering up through the deck plating—that sound. Metal scraping metal. Rhythmic now. Almost like a cry.

They burned into the black, and the *Patience* followed.
step_outputs.9:
# Chapter 4: Pursuit

Two hours in and Reese's hands ached from holding the stick. The asteroid belt near the Vicksburg mining colony was a graveyard—dead rocks and deader equipment, navigation hazards marked on no official charts. She'd run security escorts through here in her Orion days. Knew which gaps were wide enough, which sensors glitched near the iron deposits.

The *Patience* knew them too.

"Still on us," Kellen said from engineering. "Point-seven kilometers and holding."

"They're not trying to close," Reese said. Her mouth was dry. "Just keeping visual."

"Waiting for us to make a mistake." Kellen rerouted something, and the engine whine dropped half a pitch. "Or run out of fuel. We're burning at hundred-five percent. Two hours, maybe three before we're coasting."

Tav was hunched over sensors, their face lit blue by the display. "Jump point Gamma is forty minutes out. If we can reach it—"

"They'll reach it same time we do," Kellen said. "Jump calculations take eight minutes minimum. They'll have boarded us in six."

The ship shuddered as Reese threaded between two tumbling rocks, each the size of a hab module. The *Patience* followed, elegant and unhurried.

"Options," Reese said.

"Fresh out," Kellen said.

Tav's console beeped. "Captain, I'm picking up—there's something in the cargo bay. Movement."

Reese didn't take her eyes off the viewscreen. "It's secured?"

"The crate's locked down, but—" Another beep. "Temperature's spiking. It's trying to get out."

"Can it?"

Silence. Then Tav, quietly: "I don't know."

Kellen swore. "Someone needs to check on it."

"I'll go," Tav said, already unbuckling.

"Make it fast," Reese said. "And don't open anything."

Tav disappeared down the ladder. Reese angled the *Comet* into a debris field, the kind of navigation that required full attention—threading between rocks and frozen equipment husks while the *Patience* paced them like a shark.

"You really hate them that much?" Kellen asked.

Reese's jaw tightened. "What?"

"Orion. You really hate them enough to burn eighteen thousand credits and get us all killed?"

"Probably not."

"Then why?"

Reese pulled up, hard, clearing a rock by meters. The ship groaned. "You want me to say something noble? I don't have it. I looked at that crate and I couldn't—" She stopped. "I'm not having this conversation while flying through a debris field."

"Fair." Kellen worked in silence for a minute. Then: "I meant what I said. About my family."

"I know."

"Davey's medication costs six hundred a month. Station clinic keeps raising prices. My sister works two shifts and it's not enough."

"I know, Kellen."

"So what's the plan? We can't run forever. We can't dock anywhere corporate. Where does this end?"

Reese didn't have an answer. She'd made the choice to run on instinct and spite, and now they were here—burning fuel they couldn't replace, running from a better ship, with nowhere to go.

Twenty years with Orion and she'd never questioned an order. Six years free and she still didn't know how to make the right call.

"I don't know," she said.

Kellen didn't respond. The engine whine filled the bridge, too loud, too hot.

Tav came back up the ladder fast, breathing hard. "It's hurt."

"What?"

"The creature. It was thrashing in the restraints, trying to get out. There's—" Tav's hands were shaking. "It's bleeding. This luminescent stuff, all over the crate. And it keeps making that sound."

"Is the crate compromised?" Kellen asked.

"No, it's secure, but—Captain, we can't just leave it like that."

"We can and we will," Reese said. "Strap in. We're trying for the jump point."

Tav strapped in, but their face had that look—the one Reese was learning to recognize. Stubborn. Moral. Exhausting.

Thirty minutes to the jump point. The *Patience* still following. Reese pushed the engines to hundred-eight percent and listened to Kellen's running commentary of everything that was about to break.

They reached the jump point with the corporate ship point-four kilometers back. Reese cut engines, let them coast. "Tav, calculations. Now."

Tav's hands flew. "Running it. Eight minutes for full verification, maybe six if we skip secondary checks—"

"Skip them."

The *Patience* hailed again. Same bored voice: "*Comet*, you're making this harder than it needs to be. We will disable your engines if you attempt to jump. Stand down."

Reese muted it. "Kellen, jump drive status?"

"Spooling up. Five minutes." A pause. "Reese, something's wrong."

"Define wrong."

"The drive's not engaging. Error cascade, system fault, I'm trying to isolate it—" Kellen's fingers moved frantically. "It's the tracker. The one in the crate. It's not just transmitting location."

"What's it doing?"

"Disruption field. Low-grade, but enough to prevent jump engagement. As long as it's active, we can't jump."

Tav turned. "Can you disable it?"

"Not from here. Have to do it manually. In the cargo bay."

Reese calculated. The *Patience* was closing. Four minutes, maybe less before they were in boarding range.

"The tracker's in the crate lining?" she asked.

"Yeah. Embedded."

"So we space the crate."

Silence on the bridge. Then Tav: "The creature's still in there."

"I know."

"We can't just—"

"We can pull it out first," Kellen said. "Get it out of the crate, space the crate with the tracker. Then we can jump."

"It's in restraints," Tav said. "And it's injured and scared. You want to just grab it?"

"You have a better idea?"

The proximity alarm shifted pitch. The *Patience* was charging weapons—not to fire, Reese knew. Warning shot across their bow. Show of force.

Reese unbuckled. "We're out of time. Tav, you got two minutes to get that thing out of the crate. Kellen, prep the cargo airlock. Soon as the tracker's gone, jump us anywhere that isn't here."

"Where are you going?" Kellen asked.

"To help." She headed for the ladder, then stopped. Looked back at Tav. "It's already hurt you once. It'll probably try again. Don't be stupid."

Tav was already moving. "Wasn't planning on it."

They descended into the cargo bay together. The creature's sound—metal on metal—echoed off the bulkheads, louder than before. Desperate or angry, Reese couldn't tell.

She checked her sidearm. Still loaded.

Just in case.
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter 5: Specimen

Reese left after thirty seconds. "You got this?" Not really a question. Then she was gone, back up the ladder to help Kellen prep the jump.

Tav stood alone in the cargo bay with the thing in the crate.

The creature had stopped thrashing. It was watching them—silver-black eyes tracking movement, head tilted at an angle that shouldn't have been possible with that bone structure. The bioluminescent patches on its skin pulsed erratic, blue-green light casting shadows that moved wrong.

"Okay," Tav said. Their voice sounded too loud. "So. I need you to move."

The creature didn't move.

"We're trying to help. I think. Maybe you don't care. Maybe you'd rather take your chances in vacuum than with us, and honestly, fair—"

They stopped. Talking to it was stupid. It probably couldn't understand, and even if it could, why would it trust them? They were the ones who'd kept it locked in a box.

But it was looking at them. Really looking, not the way an animal looks. The way a person looks when they're deciding something about you.

Tav stepped closer. The creature's muscles tensed—they could see it through the skin, coiled and ready. Those restraints wouldn't hold if it really wanted out.

"I'm going to come closer," Tav said. "I'm not going to hurt you. I need to get you out so we can—so we can get rid of the tracker. You understand tracker? Probably not. The thing that's—"

They were babbling. Always babbled when nervous. Back in university, their roommate used to throw things at them to make them shut up during exam week. That was before the gambling, before everything went wrong, when Tav still thought they had a future that looked like their classmates' futures—research positions and publications and a life that made sense.

Felt like a century ago.

Tav pulled out their ration bar—compressed protein and grains, tasted like salted cardboard—and broke off a piece. Held it out.

"Hungry?"

The creature's eyes tracked to the food. Its nostrils flared—did it have nostrils? The slits in what passed for a face, anyway. It made a sound, softer than the metal scraping. Almost curious.

"Yeah, it's not great," Tav said. "But it's what I've got."

They tossed the piece. It landed near the creature's—paw? Hand? Too many fingers, joints bending wrong. The creature looked at it. Sniffed. Turned its head away.

"Okay, you've got standards. I respect that."

Kellen's voice crackled over comms: "Tav, we got three minutes. Maybe less."

"Working on it."

Tav stepped closer. One meter away now. Close enough to see the luminescent blood—thicker than human blood, almost gelatinous—smeared on the restraints. Close enough to smell it—ozone and something else, mineral, like licking a battery.

"Listen," they said quietly. "I know you're scared. I know you don't know what's happening. But those people—" They gestured vaguely upward, meaning the *Patience*, meaning Orion, meaning all of it. "They want to hurt you. We're trying not to. That's something, right?"

The creature's eyes met theirs. Held.

And Tav felt something. Not telepathy, nothing that clean. More like standing near someone who's crying and feeling your own throat tighten. Emotion without words. Fear. Exhaustion. Something else—defiance, maybe. Or hate.

"You understand," Tav whispered. "Don't you?"

The creature moved. Slow, deliberate. Reached out one limb—the front left, least injured—and touched the restraint buckle near its chest. Looked at Tav. Back to the buckle.

"You want—" Tav's heart was hammering. "Okay. Okay, I'm going to try."

They knelt. The creature tensed but didn't lunge. Up close it was worse—skin wrong texture, breath that came in syncopated rhythms, body heat that felt too cold. Alien in a way that made Tav's hindbrain scream wrong wrong wrong.

But its eyes were scared.

Tav's hands shook as they worked the buckle. Corporate restraints, designed not to be opened from inside. But Tav had studied xenobiology for two years before dropping out, had read papers on specimen containment, knew how these worked.

The first restraint came free. The creature pulled its limb back, flexed it. Made that soft sound again.

"Yeah," Tav said. "Better?"

They freed the others, one by one. The creature didn't attack. Just watched them work, patient or calculating, Tav couldn't tell which.

The last restraint fell away. The creature stood—unfolded, really, limbs extending in ways that bent geometry. It was bigger than Tav thought. Not quite as tall, but broader, and those limbs had reach.

It looked at them. Tav held very still.

Then it moved past them, out of the crate. Limped—one of its back legs was injured—to the corner of the cargo bay. Turned around three times, like a dog settling into a bed. Curled up, wrapping its limbs around itself.

Tav exhaled. "Kellen, we're clear. Space it."

"Airlock cycle initiating."

The cargo airlock clanged open. Tav and Kellen had already positioned the crate on the hauler dolly. Tav pushed it—lighter now without the creature—into the lock. Hit the cycle.

The outer door opened. The crate tumbled into vacuum, spinning away into the black, carrying its tracker into nothing.

The creature made a sound—sharp, distressed. The metallic shriek from before, but worse. It uncurled, pressed against the bulkhead, staring at the airlock like something had been ripped away.

"It's okay," Tav said. "It's just the box. You're safe."

Were they lying? They didn't know.

Kellen's voice: "Jump drive engaging. Brace."

The *Comet* lurched. Reality twisted, and then they were in hyperspace—that particular silence, that sense of being nowhere.

Tav slid down to sit against the wall. The creature was still pressed to the bulkhead, breathing hard, those luminescent patches flaring and dimming. It looked at Tav. Tav looked back.

"We're going to figure this out," Tav said. To the creature, to themselves. "I don't know how, but—"

The creature turned away. Curled back up, facing the wall.

Tav sat there in the cargo bay, alone but not alone, and tried not to think about what came next.
step_outputs.11:
# Chapter 6: Hyperspace

Day one, Kellen tried to feed it.

"Look," they said, crouching three meters from where the creature had wedged itself between two cargo containers. "You need to eat. Don't care if you like it or not."

They'd brought a bowl of protein paste mixed with water. Standard rations, bland enough that most species could metabolize it. The creature watched Kellen push the bowl forward, then made that scraping sound—quieter now, but still wrong—and turned its head away.

"Suit yourself." Kellen left the bowl and climbed back to the main deck.

Two hours later, Tav tried. Sat on the deck, cross-legged, holding out different options. Protein bar. Dried fruit. Water. The creature sniffed each one, rejected them all.

"Okay, so you're picky." Tav leaned back against the bulkhead. Their neck hurt from watching the creature at an angle, and they'd been down here most of the day. "The files said you eat metal compounds. We don't really have—"

The creature moved. Fast. Scuttled past Tav—who flinched hard—to the corner where Kellen's spare parts bin sat. It nosed through the bin, pulled out a corroded power coupling. Bit down.

The sound of metal crunching between teeth that shouldn't be able to do that.

"Oh," Tav said weakly. "Okay."

The creature ate three couplings and part of a broken circuit board before retreating to its corner. Tav climbed to the bridge and told Kellen, who stared at them, then at the engineering readouts.

"It ate the backup power couplings," Kellen said flatly.

"I didn't know it would—"

"We're down to three-quarters engine efficiency. If something breaks, we've got nothing to replace it with."

"I'll watch it," Tav said. "Make sure it doesn't—"

"Yeah. You do that." Kellen turned back to their console.

Tav went back down. The creature was watching them from its corner, eyes reflecting the cargo bay lights. Tav couldn't read its expression. Didn't know if it had expressions.

"We're trying," they said quietly. "Just—try back, okay?"

The creature blinked its inner eyelids and looked away.

---

Day two, none of them slept right.

The creature wouldn't stay put. It moved through the ship—cargo bay to lower deck to corridor outside the cabins. Not aggressive, just restless. Pacing. That limp pronounced, dragging its injured leg.

They took watches. Kellen took first shift, sitting in the corridor with a tablet and a wrench. Reese took second. Tav took third, though they didn't sleep during Reese's shift, just lay in their bunk listening to the creature's claws clicking on deck plating.

During Reese's watch, she sat in the cargo bay with her flask. The creature was near the aft bulkhead, curled but not sleeping. She could see its eyes open, watching the nothing of hyperspace through the porthole.

She drank. The whiskey was cheap, burned going down. Same brand she'd drunk after bad days at Orion. After retrievals that went wrong. After Jericho-7.

"You're going to get us killed," she said to the creature.

It turned its head. Looked at her.

"Yeah, you." She took another drink. "Eighteen thousand credits. Kellen's got a nephew needs medicine. Tav's probably got debts they're not talking about. Me, I just need to keep this ship flying. And here you are."

The creature made a sound—not the metallic scrape, something else. Lower. Almost inquisitive.

"Don't give me that," Reese said. "You think I'm doing this for you? I'm doing it because I'm tired of being the person who hands things over. Doesn't make us friends."

The creature kept watching her. Reese held the stare for thirty seconds before looking away. Hated that it felt like losing.

She drank until the flask was empty, then sat there in silence with the creature until Tav came to relieve her.

"Any trouble?" Tav asked.

"No." Reese stood, swayed slightly. "It's your problem now."

She left Tav sitting against the wall, three meters from the creature, both of them awake in the humming quiet of hyperspace.

---

Day two, afternoon by ship's clock, Tav pulled up the files again.

They'd copied everything from the crate's datapad before they spaced it. Manifests, protocols, research notes. Most of it was redacted—corporate security—but Tav had learned some things at university before they'd dropped out. Knew how to read around redactions. Find patterns.

Species designation: *Hadrian-9 origin, unclassified genus.*

Hadrian-9. Tav pulled up the star charts. Hadrian system, ninth planet. Flagged in the database: *Quarantined. Biohazard. No approach.*

They dug deeper. Found the incident reports from twenty years ago. The war—the one their parents never talked about, the one that ended before Tav was born. Corporate expansion into frontier systems, resistance from colonies that didn't want to be owned. It got ugly.

Hadrian-9 got ugliest.

Core fleet glassed it. Orbital bombardment, sustained for seventy-two hours. Everything on the surface burned, then melted, then turned to glass. The reports said it was necessary. Said the planet had been developing bioweapons. Said it was a threat.

No survivors reported.

But here was this creature. With Hadrian-9 listed as its origin.

Tav scrolled through the specimen notes. *Captured: Hadrian system, asteroid belt near former ninth planet. Solo. Juvenile. No others detected despite extensive search.*

Last of its kind. Maybe.

*Intelligence assessment: Moderate to high. Problem-solving observed. Tool use suspected but not confirmed. Communication method unclear. Empathic or telepathic capabilities possible.*

Tav looked up from the tablet. The creature was across the cargo bay, pulling at a loose deck panel. Trying to get underneath it.

"Hey," Tav said. "Don't do that."

The creature stopped. Looked at them. Went back to pulling at the panel.

Tav stood, walked over. "Seriously, you can't—"

The creature got the panel up. Underneath: wiring conduits, coolant lines, ship's guts. It reached in with one limb, delicate despite the wrong joints, and touched a wire.

"No no no, that's—" Tav grabbed for the creature's limb.

The creature yanked back. Fast. That metallic shriek, defensive. It scrambled backward, pressed against the bulkhead, breathing hard.

Tav's hands were shaking. "Sorry. I'm sorry. Just—you can't touch those. It'll hurt you. Or break the ship. Or both."

The creature watched them. Didn't move.

Tav slowly replaced the panel, secured it. "The ship keeps us alive. All of us. You too now, I guess. So we have to—we need you to not break it. Okay?"

The creature tilted its head. That gesture that looked almost human, almost like understanding.

Then it turned and limped back to its corner.

Tav sat on the deck and put their head in their hands. Two more days to Blacktrace. Two more days of this.

They pulled up the files again. Read the part they'd been avoiding.

*Destination: Hadrian Research Facility, Quarantine Wing. Purpose: Vivisection, genetic sampling, behavioral study, potential military application of empathic capabilities.*

*Specimen survival: Not required past initial study phase.*

Tav closed the file. Looked at the creature in its corner.

"We made the right choice," they said, quiet enough that maybe it couldn't hear. "We had to."

The creature's bioluminescent patches pulsed, unsteady as a failing heartbeat.
step_outputs.12:
# Chapter 7: Burn the Boats

They dropped out of hyperspace to find Blacktrace Station crawling with corporate ships.

"Shit," Kellen said. "Shit shit shit."

Three vessels in dock, two on patrol. All corporate security configuration. The *Patience* wasn't among them, but its sister ships were, and they'd already pinged the *Comet*'s approach.

"How?" Tav's voice was too high. "How did they know we'd come here?"

"Process of elimination," Reese said. Her hands were already moving, pulling up alternate routes. "We can't dock anywhere corporate. Blacktrace is the closest independent station. They waited." She felt stupid. Should have seen this.

The hail came through. Different voice this time, female, almost friendly: "Freighter *Dusty Comet*, this is security vessel *Meridian*. You're carrying stolen corporate property. Dock at bay seven and surrender it peacefully, or we will disable your ship and take it by force. You have five minutes to decide."

Reese muted the channel. "Fuel status?"

"Thirty percent," Kellen said. "Maybe enough for one jump, if we don't mind coming out of hyperspace dead in the water."

"Anywhere that isn't here, then." Reese started calculating. "Tav, I need jump coordinates to—"

"Captain, there's another ship." Tav's hands shook on the console. "Just appeared on sensors. Not corporate. Independent signature, but it's—it's hailing us. Private channel."

Reese's gut clenched. "Put it through."

The voice that came through was painfully familiar. Warm. Amused.

"Reese. It's been a while."

She closed her eyes. "Kade."

"You sound surprised. You run corporate cargo, burn half the sector, you think I wouldn't hear about it?" A pause. "I'm hurt you didn't call."

Kellen was looking at her. Reese ignored them. "What do you want?"

"Same thing as everyone else. The specimen. Orion's offering two hundred thousand for its return. But I'm a reasonable person—you hand it over to me, I'll split the finder's fee. Forty-sixty, and you walk away clean."

"Forty percent."

"You're the one who stole it. I'm the one doing you a favor."

Tav was staring at her. Reese kept her face neutral. "Not interested."

"Reese." Kade's voice shifted, less amused. "You know how this ends. Corporate has six ships here. I've got one. You've got a cargo hauler with failing engines and no fuel. You can't run. You can't fight. You can hand over the specimen and take the money, or they'll take it and you'll get nothing. Possibly get dead."

"I'll take my chances."

"With what? That crew of yours?" A pause. "I've been watching the station feeds. You've got a kid navigator and an engineer. What are you going to do, talk them to death?"

"Better than what I'd do to you." 

Kade laughed. "Still angry about Jericho-7? I followed orders, Reese. Same as you were supposed to."

"I'm done talking." She reached for the channel close.

"Wait." Kade's voice went serious. "I looked at the manifest. You know what that thing is? Hadrian-9 survivor. The planet we glassed. Command thinks it might have empathic abilities—maybe that's how it survived. Maybe it made our people hesitate." A pause. "You're protecting something that might have killed our soldiers, Reese. Doesn't that bother you?"

It did. Reese hated that it did.

"We glassed a planet," she said. "Killed everything on it. Maybe they had a right to fight back."

"That's sedition talk."

"Then arrest me. Oh wait—you're not corporate anymore either. Just a bounty hunter chasing money."

"And you're just a cargo hauler playing hero. At least I'm honest about what I am." Kade's tone hardened. "Four minutes, Reese. Then corporate stops being polite."

The channel cut.

Kellen spoke first. "Who the hell was that?"

"Old colleague." Reese was calculating vectors, fuel consumption, probability of success. All low. "We worked together at Orion. They stayed when I left."

"They know you," Tav said quietly. "How you think."

"Yeah."

The corporate ships were moving into intercept positions. Coordinated. Professional. Reese recognized the pattern—containment formation, designed to box in a target without destroying it. They wanted the creature intact.

"Kellen, how much engine do we have?"

"Not enough."

"That's not a number."

"Redline for maybe thirty minutes before something critical melts. After that we're coasting."

Thirty minutes. Six ships. Reese ran the scenarios. All of them ended with the *Comet* disabled and boarded.

A sound from below—the creature, that metallic shriek. It had been quiet during the jump, but now it was agitated. Maybe it felt the danger. Maybe it was just scared.

"We could surrender," Tav said. Nobody looked at them. "I'm just—I'm saying it's an option."

"No it isn't," Kellen said.

Tav's face flushed. "You said this was stupid. You said we shouldn't have run. Now you—"

"I said it was stupid. Didn't say I wanted to give up." Kellen's hands moved across engineering controls. "We ran. We're in it. So we finish it."

Reese looked at them. Kellen met her eyes, shrugged. "Don't make it a thing."

The proximity alarms started screaming. Corporate ships closing.

"Tav," Reese said. "Can you fly?"

"I—what?"

"Can. You. Fly."

"I'm a navigator, not a—"

"You've got simulator hours. You've got hands. Get on the stick."

Tav scrambled to the pilot's seat. Reese moved to weapons—the *Comet* had two defensive guns, designed for pirates and debris, nothing like what they were facing.

"Kellen, reroute everything non-essential to engines and shields. Life support on minimum."

"We'll freeze."

"Rather freeze than get boarded. Do it."

The ship lurched as Tav grabbed controls. "Sorry, sorry—"

"Just fly. Toward the debris field, there—" Reese highlighted it on their display. "Get us into cover."

Tav burned hard. Too hard—the *Comet* wasn't built for combat maneuvers. Something in the lower deck clanged loose. The creature's shrieking got louder.

Corporate ships opened fire. Warning shots, close enough to feel.

"They're not trying to hit us," Kellen said.

"Yet," Reese said. She fired back—not at the ships, at the space between them and the station. Made them adjust course. "Tav, tighter. Use the debris."

Tav pulled the ship into a broken satellite's shadow. Their hands were white-knuckled on the stick. One of the corporate vessels overshot, had to burn retro to compensate.

"Good," Reese said. "Again. That wreck at two o'clock."

They played cat and mouse through the debris field. Tav was clumsy but learning fast, using the *Comet*'s smaller size against the bigger ships. Kellen kept shields up, rerouting power as systems failed.

Then Kade's ship appeared, fast and clean, cutting off their escape vector.

"Last chance, Reese," over the comms. "I don't want to hurt you."

Reese fired on them. Missed—Kade dodged easy.

"Okay," Kade said. "Had to try."

Kade's ship opened fire. Not warning shots. The *Comet* shuddered as a round took their port shield. Tav yelped, jerked the stick, nearly rammed a derelict container.

"I can't—there's too many—" Tav's voice was cracking.

Reese looked at the display. Six corporate ships, Kade's ship, all closing. Debris field ending fifty kilometers ahead. After that, open space. No cover. No options.

"Kellen," she said. "That jump you mentioned. The one that leaves us dead in the water."

"Yeah?"

"Pick somewhere far. Coordinates now."

"Reese, we won't have enough power to—"

"Now."

Kellen's hands flew. "Coordinates locked. This is insane."

"Tav, when I say jump, you jump. Don't think. Just do it."

Another hit. Starboard shield failed. Alarm klaxons screaming. The creature below was throwing itself against the cargo bay walls—Reese could hear it through the deck plating.

Kade's voice: "Don't be stupid, Reese. You can't jump with shields failing. You'll tear the ship apart."

Maybe. Probably.

Reese looked at her crew. Tav terrified but holding steady. Kellen already rerouting power to jump drives, face set.

"Three years I worked with you," Kade said. "I know you. You're practical. You don't die for lost causes."

Reese opened the channel. "Yeah. Guess I changed."

She closed it. Looked at Tav. "Jump."

Tav hit the drive.

Reality twisted. The *Comet* screamed. Something exploded in engineering—Kellen swearing, smoke filling the bridge. The creature's shriek hit a pitch that made Reese's teeth ache.

Then silence.

Hyperspace. They were in hyperspace.

Systems were failing. Power flickering. Kellen was putting out a small fire with their jacket. Tav was shaking so hard they could barely unbuckle.

But they were alive.

And everywhere behind them, every contact, every safe port, every bridge back to the life Reese had built—

Burned.
step_outputs.13:
# Chapter 8: New Heading

The *Dusty Comet* drifted for six hours before Kellen got life support back online.

Reese spent those hours in the bridge wearing two jackets and watching her breath fog. Tav was in their cabin, door closed. The creature was silent in the cargo bay—either dead or conserving energy, Reese didn't know which.

When warmth finally cycled through the vents, Kellen climbed to the bridge. They had a burn across their left hand, bandaged badly, and their face was streaked with soot.

"Damage report," Reese said.

"Where do you want me to start?" Kellen sat heavily in the engineering console. "Port engine's gone. Starboard's running at forty percent. Shields are slag. We've got life support and basic maneuvering. That's it."

"Jump drive?"

"Fried. We're not jumping again without a full rebuild and parts we can't afford."

Reese nodded. She'd expected worse. "Where are we?"

"Uncharted space, far edge of the frontier. No stations within three weeks' burn. Assuming we had fuel. Which we don't." Kellen pulled up the star chart. "We've got maybe enough fuel to coast to this system here. Undesignated. Four planets, one possibly habitable. Maybe."

"And if it's not?"

"Then we freeze or suffocate. Whichever comes first."

They sat in silence. Outside the viewscreen, stars burned cold and distant. Reese thought about Kade, the corporate ships, Blacktrace Station. Everything behind them. Everything gone.

"So," Kellen said. "Was it worth it?"

Reese didn't answer.

"My nephew needs medicine. Six hundred credits a month. I won't be sending that anymore." Kellen's voice was flat. "My sister's going to have to figure something else out. Hope the station clinic has charity rates."

"Kellen—"

"I'm not done." They turned to look at her. "You made a call. I didn't agree. Still don't. But I stayed. That's on me." A pause. "Just wanted you to know what it cost."

Reese met their eyes. "I know."

"Good." Kellen stood, winced. "I'm going to sleep for twelve hours. Try not to blow anything else up."

They left. Reese sat alone in the bridge and tried not to calculate all the ways she'd destroyed her crew's lives.

---

Tav found the creature in the cargo bay, curled in the corner where coolant had leaked. The puddle had frozen during the six hours without heat. The creature was lying in it, shivering—its whole body contracting and releasing in rhythmic spasms.

"Hey," Tav said. "You okay?"

Stupid question. It wasn't okay. None of them were okay.

Tav grabbed an emergency blanket from the supply locker. Approached slowly. The creature watched them come, made a sound—not the metallic shriek, something smaller. Hurt.

"I'm going to put this on you," Tav said. "For warmth. Don't bite me."

They draped the blanket over the creature. It flinched but didn't pull away. Tav sat nearby, not quite close enough to touch.

"We're kind of screwed," Tav said. "In case you were wondering. No fuel, no jump drive, middle of nowhere. But hey—nobody's trying to vivisect you. So that's something."

The creature's luminescent patches pulsed weakly under the blanket.

"I looked up your planet," Tav continued. "Hadrian-9. Read what happened. What we—what humanity did to it." They pulled their knees up to their chest. "You've got every right to hate us. Probably do hate us. But we're trying. I'm trying."

The creature shifted. Turned its head to look at Tav directly.

"I ran away from home," Tav said. Hadn't meant to say it. "Got into debt. Gambling. Stupid, self-destructive, classic screwup behavior. My family doesn't know where I am. Probably think I'm dead. Might be better if they did." They wiped their face. "I wanted to be good at something. Wanted to matter. Thought maybe helping you—"

They stopped. The creature was still watching.

"Sorry," Tav said. "You probably don't care about my issues."

The creature made that soft sound again. Extended one limb—slowly, deliberately—and touched Tav's boot. Just rested it there, claws against worn synthetic.

Tav's breath caught. "Yeah?"

The creature didn't pull away.

They sat like that for a long time, while the ship drifted through nothing, and the cold slowly retreated.

---

Day three in nowhere, Reese called a crew meeting.

They gathered in the galley—the only place big enough. The creature stayed in the cargo bay. Reese didn't invite it. Wasn't sure how you invited something that might not understand language.

"Options," Reese said. "We can try for the undesignated system. Maybe find somewhere to land, send a distress beacon. Maybe someone picks it up before we die."

"What about the creature?" Tav asked.

"What about it?"

"We can't just—if we send a distress beacon, whoever comes will find it. Might be corporate."

"Might be," Reese agreed. "Might be independent. Might be pirates. Might be nobody." She looked at both of them. "We've got no good options. Just less-bad ones."

Kellen spoke up. "Could try to make it to the outer colonies. Three weeks' burn if we had fuel. Could siphon fuel from the creature's crate—oh wait, we spaced that."

"There's another option," Tav said quietly. "We could call Kade."

The galley went silent.

"Forty percent of two hundred thousand," Tav continued. "That's eighty thousand credits. Enough to repair the ship. Enough to pay Kellen's nephew's medicine for years. Enough to—"

"No," Reese said.

"I'm just saying—"

"I said no."

"Why?" Tav's voice rose. "Because of your pride? Your grudge against your old boss? We made our point. We ran. We saved it. Now we're going to die out here because you won't—"

"I won't hand over something alive to be cut apart," Reese said. "That's the line. You want off this ship, I can't stop you. But that's the line."

Tav's jaw worked. Then: "I don't want off the ship."

"Then we agree."

Kellen sighed. "Alright. We try for the undesignated system. See if we can land without crashing. Figure out the rest after."

"Agreed," Reese said.

They sat there in the galley, three people in a dying ship at the edge of mapped space, with a creature in the hold that might be the last of its kind, and no plan beyond survival.

"For what it's worth," Kellen said, "this is still the stupidest thing I've ever done."

"Noted," Reese said.

"Just wanted that on record."

---

Tav took first watch that night. Sat in the cargo bay with the creature, which had moved from its corner to the spot near the porthole. It was looking out at the stars—the unfamiliar constellations of uncharted space.

"We're going to find somewhere," Tav said. "I don't know where. But somewhere."

The creature's bioluminescent patches pulsed. Slow. Almost steady.

Tav pulled out their tablet. Looked at the message draft they'd been writing for two months. *Dear Mom and Mama, I'm sorry I left. I'm sorry I didn't tell you. I'm sorry for everything. I'm—*

They deleted it. Started over.

*I'm alive. I'm trying to be better. I'll contact you when I can.*

They saved it. Didn't send it. Not yet. Maybe when they reached somewhere with a beacon. Maybe never.

The creature shifted, settling into a position that looked almost comfortable. Tav leaned against the bulkhead and closed their eyes.

Above them, Reese was in the bridge, plotting a course toward a maybe-habitable planet in an unnamed system. The coordinates were set. In six hours they'd start the burn.

The *Dusty Comet* drifted through the dark, broken and barely flying. Inside: three humans who'd chosen the harder path, and one creature that might be the last of its kind.

No one knew what came next.

But they'd find out together.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

**Characterization:**
Reese is rendered with impressive economy. The detail of her cutting off both Reed and Kellen with "We don't get paid to notice" establishes her pragmatic authority without announcement. The "small, mean satisfaction of silence, immediately followed by the usual guilt she ignored" is a precise psychological note—it shows her self-awareness without making her sympathetic in a cheap way. Her tea-and-solitaire detail lands naturally. Tav's nervous chatter, bitten nails, and the hoodie falling off the shoulder are well-chosen physical details that communicate character without laboring the point. Kellen's competence and quiet skepticism ("I'm reminding you I said something") is efficiently established. All three characters are consistent with their profiles.

**Dialogue:**
The dialogue is largely excellent—clipped, subtext-laden, functional. "We don't get paid to notice" / "We don't get paid to ask questions either" has a nice parallel structure that reveals Reese's management style. Kellen's "Fair enough" after the hair-splitting exchange is perfectly calibrated. The dialogue avoids the trap of characters explaining themselves. The one slightly weaker moment is Tav's "So, uh, we don't usually do corporate contracts" line, which is a bit on-the-nose as an exposition delivery mechanism, though it's saved by being immediately shut down.

**Prose Quality:**
The prose is clean and controlled. Sentence variation is good—fragments appear naturally in moments of observation. The physical details are specific without being overwrought: "recycled atmosphere and someone frying synthetic protein," "tailored but trying not to look it." These are earned observations, not decorative. The biohazard markers "under the corporate logo. Faint, but they're there" is a nice structural beat.

**Pacing and Structure:**
The chapter follows its plan closely—establishing the shady job, the crew dynamics, the financial desperation, the biohazard detail, and ending on the discovery hook. The transition from dock to six hours in is handled efficiently. The chapter doesn't overstay its welcome.

**Weaknesses:**
The chapter is competent but occasionally safe. The "corporate contractor you could tell by the way he wore his jacket" observation is a bit familiar as a character-typing shortcut. The ending hook—Tav's voice over comms—is effective but the chapter plan itself acknowledged this was a deliberate choice to delay the "something's moving" line, and the execution is solid if not surprising. The chapter doesn't take many risks; it's executing a plan well rather than transcending it. The "small, mean satisfaction" line is the most psychologically interesting moment, and there could be more of that texture throughout.

**Forced Poetry/Purple Prose:**
Essentially absent. The prose is restrained and functional. No metaphors that don't land, no reaching for profundity.

**Faithfulness to Plan:**
Very high. The chapter hits every planned beat: the biohazard markers, Kellen's skepticism, Tav being shut down, the financial stakes, running silent, and the modified ending hook (Tav says "there's a sound" rather than the exact "something's moving" line, which is actually a better choice—more uncertain, more atmospheric).

**Overall:**
This is a well-executed opening chapter that establishes tone, character, and stakes with economy and confidence. It's not a masterpiece—it doesn't surprise or transcend its genre in ways that would push it to the highest tier—but it's genuinely good work: controlled, specific, and true to its intentions.

[Analysis]

Chapter 3 is a tightly constructed piece that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

**Strengths:**

The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The three-way debate structure works: Kellen's pragmatism, Tav's idealism, and Reese's complicated middle ground are all rendered with appropriate texture. The Kellen-Tav confrontation over Davey's medication is the chapter's strongest moment—it's specific, it stings, and it doesn't resolve cleanly. Kellen isn't wrong, and the chapter doesn't pretend otherwise.

Reese's internal monologue about Jericho-7 is handled with appropriate restraint. The plan called for her to think about her corporate past, and the writing delivers this without over-explaining. "Retrieval. Like the things they were taking weren't alive, weren't aware, weren't screaming in frequencies that made your teeth ache." This is effective—it earns the darkness without wallowing.

The dialogue is generally strong. The exchange rhythms feel natural, clipped, and character-appropriate. Reese's "Don't make this noble. I just hate those bastards more than I like money" lands exactly as intended—it's the chapter's thesis line and it works because it's been earned by the preceding internal conflict. The muting of the corporate channel and the professional-bored hail establishes the corporate antagonist efficiently.

Character consistency is high. Reese is decisive but not heroic, motivated by spite as much as conscience. Kellen is loyal but unconvinced, practical without being villainous. Tav is earnest and slightly preachy, flinching at Kellen's accurate shot about not having dependents.

**Weaknesses:**

The Hadrian-9 revelation—that the creature may be the last of its species—is a significant escalation that the plan mentions but the chapter handles somewhat mechanically. "Thought extinct," Reese said. This lands a bit flatly; it's important information delivered without quite enough weight. The moment passes quickly when it might have benefited from a beat of silence or reaction.

The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows Reese's internal state. "She thought about Jericho-7. The holding cells. The screaming." This is efficient but slightly on-the-nose. The plan called for understatement, and while this is understated compared to many approaches, it's still fairly direct emotional labeling.

Tav's "Thank you" and Reese's "Don't" exchange is good but slightly predictable—it's exactly what you'd expect from these characters in this moment. The plan warned against unsurprising beats, and this one is the most telegraphed in the chapter.

The creature's presence at the end—"that sound. Metal scraping metal. Rhythmic now. Almost like a cry."—is a nice closing image but "almost like a cry" edges toward the sentimental in a way the plan explicitly wanted to avoid.

The corporate ship's name, *Patience*, is a nice touch that goes unremarked, which is the right call.

**Overall:** This is competent, disciplined writing that executes its plan faithfully. It doesn't reach for effects it can't land, and it mostly avoids the pitfalls the author identified. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element; the internal monologue is its weakest. The moral ambiguity is genuine rather than performed. It's not a masterpiece—some beats are predictable, the Hadrian-9 revelation is underplayed, and the ending image tips slightly sentimental—but it's solid, purposeful work.

[Analysis]

This chapter is a largely successful execution of an intimate, pressure-cooker scene that the plan called for. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

**Strengths:**

The chapter does what the plan intended: it slows down, gets small, and focuses on the relationship between Tav and the creature without sentimentalizing it. The creature remains genuinely alien—the description of its body heat feeling "too cold," the syncopated breathing rhythms, the ozone-and-battery smell—these are specific and effective. The creature doesn't become cute or Disney-fied; it tolerates Tav rather than bonding with them, and the chapter ends with it turning away, which is exactly the right note.

Tav's characterization is consistent with the profile. The nervous babbling, the memory flash to university and the gambling spiral, the transactional quality of their compassion ("something that needed them"), the way they project onto the creature—all of this tracks. The line "its eyes were scared" is doing real work: it's Tav's interpretation, and the reader is left uncertain whether it's accurate or projection.

The dialogue is mostly good. "Yeah, you've got standards. I respect that" is a natural, slightly self-deprecating line that fits Tav's voice. The babbling quality is well-rendered. The comms interruptions from Kellen create appropriate tension without being intrusive.

The plan called for the creature to arrange objects in a pattern (showing possible intelligence) and then urinate on the floor (practical reality). The chapter substitutes the creature touching the restraint buckle and looking at Tav—which is arguably more elegant and serves the same purpose (ambiguous intelligence) without the comic deflation. This is a reasonable creative deviation, though the plan's "ruins the moment" beat is lost.

**Weaknesses:**

The emotional interiority section—the memory of the roommate throwing things, the "felt like a century ago" reflection—is the weakest passage. It's competent but slightly generic, and the transition back to the present feels a little mechanical. The plan called for a memory flash about why Tav left core systems, and this delivers it, but it doesn't land with particular force. "A life that made sense" is a bit of a cliché.

"Emotion without words. Fear. Exhaustion. Something else—defiance, maybe. Or hate." This is the most questionable moment. The plan explicitly warned against telepathy and "nothing that clean," and the chapter hedges appropriately, but the list of emotions still feels slightly too neat, too readable. The uncertainty is there but the emotions themselves are conventional.

The line "reality twisted" for the hyperspace jump is a minor purple prose moment—not egregious, but slightly lazy.

The ending—"alone but not alone"—is a small cliché that the chapter could have done without. It's the kind of phrase that sounds resonant but is actually just a familiar construction.

The plan called for the creature to eat Tav's ration bar (after initially rejecting it). In the chapter, it sniffs, rejects, and then... doesn't take it. The plan said "sniffs, rejects, then takes"—the chapter cuts the final beat, which was meant to show a small, grudging connection. This is a minor loss.

**Overall:**

This is solid, controlled writing that mostly achieves its goals. It's not a masterpiece—the interiority is occasionally generic, there are small clichés—but it's disciplined, tonally consistent, and true to both the characters and the plan. The creature remains genuinely unsettling without being cartoonishly monstrous. Tav's compassion reads as both genuine and slightly deluded, which is exactly right.

[Analysis]

Chapter 6 is a vignette-structured chapter covering three days in hyperspace, and it largely succeeds at what the writing plan set out to do: create pressure-cooker tension through mundane friction rather than action. Let me assess it carefully across the metrics.

**Strengths:**

The chapter's greatest achievement is its restraint. The dialogue is consistently good—clipped, subtext-laden, and character-specific. Kellen's "Yeah. You do that" is exactly right: dismissive without being cartoonish, conveying resentment through economy. Reese's monologue to the creature ("Don't give me that. You think I'm doing this for you?") is the chapter's best scene—it reveals character through action and speech without ever becoming a confession or declaration. The line "Hated that it felt like losing" is earned and precise.

The creature is handled well. It's not cute. It eats power couplings, it ignores food, it pulls at deck panels, it snaps when touched. The bioluminescent patches pulsing "unsteady as a failing heartbeat" at the end is one of the few metaphors in the chapter, and it lands because it's doing double work—describing the creature and echoing the chapter's emotional stakes.

The structure follows the plan faithfully: Day One feeding scene, Day Two sleep/watch scene, Day Two research scene. The discovery about Hadrian-9 and the creature being potentially the last of its kind is well-integrated—it doesn't feel like an info-dump because Tav's process of reading around redactions is shown, and the emotional weight lands in the final beat.

**Weaknesses:**

The chapter is competent but rarely surprising. The beats are predictable: creature won't eat ship food, eats metal instead; crew can't sleep; Reese drinks and talks to the creature; Tav finds devastating information in the files. Each beat is executed well, but none of them subvert expectation in interesting ways. The plan called for "tonal inconsistency—some scenes darker, some unexpectedly mundane or funny," and while the power coupling scene has some dry humor, the chapter is fairly uniform in tone.

Tav's interiority section is the weakest part. The plan called for a memory flash about why they left core systems, and instead we get a fairly direct exposition of their backstory through the Hadrian-9 research. This works narratively but doesn't give us Tav's inner life as richly as promised. The line "We made the right choice. We had to." is slightly on-the-nose—it's the kind of thing a character says when the author wants to make sure the reader understands the emotional stakes, rather than trusting the scene to carry it.

The creature pulling at the deck panel and Tav stopping it is a slightly contrived scene—it exists to give them an interaction and to let Tav explain ship systems, but the creature's motivation is unclear and the scene doesn't quite earn its place. The plan mentioned the creature arranging objects in patterns (from Chapter 5's plan), and something like that would have been more interesting than the panel-pulling.

The final image—bioluminescent patches pulsing—is good but the chapter ends on Tav's self-reassurance, which is slightly softer than the plan's intention of leaving things uncomfortable and unresolved. The plan's ending for this chapter was more ambiguous.

**Character consistency:** Reese is excellent—the flask, the stare contest with the creature, the refusal to sentimentalize. Kellen is appropriately terse and resentful. Tav is mostly consistent but slightly more passive here than their profile suggests—the profile says they're preachy and self-righteous, but in this chapter they're mostly just anxious and sad.

**Prose quality:** Clean, functional, occasionally precise. No purple prose. The sentence rhythm varies appropriately. No forced metaphors. The writing is professional without being remarkable.

[Analysis]

Chapter 2 executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The writing is controlled, the pacing is deliberate, and the author demonstrates a clear understanding of what the chapter needs to accomplish: reveal the creature, establish the moral stakes, and end on a ticking-clock complication. Let me examine this carefully.

**Strengths:**

The prose is clean and purposeful. Sentence length varies appropriately—short, punchy lines during the tense reveal, slightly longer constructions during the quieter diagnostic moments. The physical description of the creature is genuinely unsettling: "Too many joints in the limbs—three, four?—and the joints bent in directions that made her eyes hurt to track." This is effective body horror that doesn't overreach. The bioluminescent patches "pulsing irregularly like a struggling heartbeat" is a good simile that earns its place.

The character work is largely faithful to the profiles. Reese's decision-making process is shown through action and internal calculation rather than declaration—she runs the numbers on insurance and decontamination costs before agreeing to open the crate. This is exactly right for her character. Her holstering the gun after recognizing the trapped-awareness in the creature's eyes is a subtle, well-executed moment that plants her eventual choice without telegraphing it. The line "Trapped. Aware of being trapped" is economical and effective.

Kellen's pragmatism comes through in their efficient, no-argument compliance once the decision is made. Tav's nervous idealism is present without being overbearing. The dialogue is generally restrained and functional—characters speak in subtext and practical concerns rather than speeches.

The creature reveal follows the plan well: unsettling proportions, shock collar, empty sedation rig, corporate manifests with a serial number instead of a species name. The detail that it's been awake for six hours in a sealed crate is quietly devastating and does moral work without editorializing.

**Weaknesses:**

The chapter is competent but rarely surprising. The beats follow the plan almost mechanically: diagnostic scan, debate about opening, override, reveal, tracker discovery, alarm. There's little that departs from expectation in a way that feels organic rather than plotted. The plan called for the creature to lunge and Tav to stumble—this happens exactly as described. The tracker reveal and proximity alarm happen exactly as planned. The chapter executes its blueprint faithfully but doesn't transcend it.

Reese's reversal—"Seal it back up. We're delivering it"—followed almost immediately by the tracker revelation that forces the next chapter's conflict, means her moral position never gets tested in this chapter. She says deliver it, then the plot removes that option. This is structurally convenient and slightly deflating.

The line "Six hours. Awake in the dark in a sealed crate" is the chapter's most emotionally manipulative moment, and it works, but it's also the most on-the-nose piece of writing here—the author telling us how to feel rather than trusting the image to do the work.

The dialogue is functional but occasionally flat. "Jesus," Tav whispered" is a placeholder reaction. "Don't shoot it" / "Not planning to" is efficient but not particularly distinctive.

The chapter plan called for Reese to draw her sidearm and establish she carries—this is done. It also called for the creature to lunge and Tav to stumble—done. The corporate manifests, serial number, destination—all executed. The tracker and proximity alarm closing—done. The chapter is a faithful execution of its blueprint.

The darkness is well-earned: the shock collar, the empty sedation rig, the "specimen" designation, the six hours awake in the dark. These accumulate without sentimentality. The chapter doesn't push for emotional response; it presents facts and lets them land.

Overall: a solid, disciplined chapter that executes its plan competently. It lacks moments of genuine surprise or prose that rises above the functional, but it doesn't commit significant errors either. The creature reveal is effective, the character voices are consistent, and the moral stakes are established without preaching.

[Analysis]

Chapter 4 of this space western demonstrates considerable craft in several areas while showing some limitations in others. Let me work through the piece systematically.

**Strengths:**

The dialogue is notably restrained and effective. The exchange between Reese and Kellen about family and money ("Davey's medication costs six hundred a month") lands with genuine weight because it's specific and unadorned. Neither character speechifies; they speak in the clipped, practical register the plan called for. Kellen's "Figured. Not angry, just tired" from the plan becomes implicit in the actual text—the resignation is shown through what's not said rather than labeled.

The pacing is well-managed. The chapter moves through action beats without lingering too long on any single moment, and the tension of the pursuit feels real. The *Patience* as a name for the corporate ship is a nice touch—understated and thematically resonant without being heavy-handed.

Reese's characterization is largely consistent with her profile. Her admission "I don't know" when Kellen asks about the plan is exactly right—she made a choice on instinct and spite and now has to live in the consequences. The line "I looked at that crate and I couldn't—" with the deliberate trailing off is good; it shows her avoidance without spelling it out.

The technical details (hundred-five percent burn, jump calculations taking eight minutes, the disruption beacon) feel grounded without being overwhelming. The asteroid belt setting works as a space western environment.

**Weaknesses:**

The chapter follows its plan quite faithfully, sometimes too faithfully—it reads slightly like a competent execution of a checklist rather than a living scene. The beats arrive in the expected order: pursuit, Kellen's family speech, failed jump, tracker revelation, decision to pull creature out. There's little that surprises within the chapter itself.

The "shark" simile ("paced them like a shark") is the most notable instance of a metaphor that feels slightly generic for the otherwise grounded prose. It's not egregious, but it's the kind of comparison that appears in a thousand thrillers.

Tav's characterization is thinner here than in the plan. They function primarily as a plot mechanism—going to check the creature, reporting back, being the moral voice—without much interiority. The plan called for Tav to go check on the creature and get close enough to remove the shock collar, but in this chapter that's deferred to the next chapter. The chapter ends with them descending together, which is a reasonable structural choice, but Tav feels underdeveloped in this section.

The creature's presence is mostly reported rather than felt. We hear about luminescent blood and the metallic sound, but the creature doesn't have the unsettling physical presence the plan promised. This is partly appropriate—they're not in the cargo bay yet—but the chapter could have done more with the sounds filtering up through the ship.

The closing line ("Just in case") is functional but slightly on-the-nose. It tells us what we already know about Reese's caution and the creature's danger without adding new texture.

The Kellen-Reese conversation is the chapter's emotional core and it works, but it's also the most conventional element—the pragmatist confronting the captain about consequences is a familiar beat. The plan called for this to feel like "not friends, exactly—work partners who trust each other," and that comes through, but the scene doesn't quite transcend its genre conventions.

**Overall Assessment:**

This is solid, competent genre writing that executes its plan faithfully. The prose is clean, the dialogue is restrained and effective, and the characters behave consistently with their profiles. It doesn't reach for effects it can't land, which is a genuine virtue. The weaknesses are mostly of omission—moments that could have been stranger, more specific, more surprising—rather than commission. It's a chapter that does its job without embarrassing itself, which in a longer work is often exactly what's needed.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

Chapter 7 is a tense, action-driven chapter that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

**Strengths:**

The chapter introduces Kade effectively as a foil for Reese—someone who stayed corporate, who knows her, who can articulate the moral complexity of the situation ("You're protecting something that might have killed our soldiers"). This is genuinely good writing: the antagonist has a point, and Reese's response ("We glassed a planet. Killed everything on it. Maybe they had a right to fight back.") is appropriately blunt without being a speech. The exchange is the chapter's strongest section.

The plan's intention to "rush through what might normally be climactic" is executed reasonably well. The combat is scrappy and desperate rather than heroic—Tav is clumsy, the ship is failing, they're outgunned. This matches the writing philosophy.

Kellen's moment—"I said it was stupid. Didn't say I wanted to give up"—is excellent. It's exactly the kind of character consistency the plan called for: not won over, not converted, just loyal to the ship. This is the chapter's best character beat.

The ending image—"Burned"—is clean and earns its weight. The final paragraph works.

**Weaknesses:**

The Kade dialogue, while functional, occasionally tips into exposition. "Hadrian-9 survivor. The planet we glassed. Command thinks it might have empathic abilities—maybe that's how it survived. Maybe it made our people hesitate." This is information delivery dressed as dialogue. A bounty hunter mid-standoff wouldn't necessarily volunteer this; it feels like the author needing to convey backstory.

The action sequence is competent but somewhat generic. "Cat and mouse through the debris field" is described efficiently but without the physical specificity that would make it visceral. We get the shape of the action but not the texture—the smells, the sounds beyond the creature, the specific sensations of a ship being pushed past its limits.

Tav's characterization is slightly thin here. They're scared and functional, which is consistent, but the plan called for Tav's idealism being "annoying, preachy"—here they're just frightened. The moment where they suggest surrender is good but underdeveloped.

The line "Guess I changed" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's the kind of declaration the writing plan explicitly said to avoid. Reese doesn't say what she means; here she does, and it's a bit on-the-nose.

The creature's presence is mostly sonic (shrieking, throwing itself against walls), which is appropriate given the chaos, but the plan's emphasis on the creature being unsettling and difficult is somewhat backgrounded.

The "Burned" ending, while effective, is slightly too neat—the metaphor of burning bridges is a familiar one, and the chapter plan's emphasis on avoiding satisfying closure is almost achieved but not quite. The final image is a bit too poetic for the gritty understatement the plan calls for.

**Consistency with Plan:**

The chapter follows the plan's broad strokes: Blacktrace is compromised, Kade appears, they can't run, they jump anyway at great cost. The plan mentioned "rush through what might normally be climactic" and this is achieved. The plan also called for "scrambling and luck" rather than heroics, which is delivered.

However, the plan doesn't mention Kade at all—this is an addition. It's a good addition that adds moral complexity, but it's a significant departure. The plan also called for the chapter to end with them having "burned every bridge," which is achieved, but the plan's specific beats (the failed plan, the desperate jump) are compressed in ways that mostly work.

**Overall:**

A solid, functional chapter that executes its action competently and has genuine character moments. Its weaknesses are in specificity of action, occasional expository dialogue, and one moment of uncharacteristic declaration from Reese. The Kade addition is a genuine creative contribution that enriches the moral stakes. Not a masterpiece, but above average work.

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this space western, and it largely succeeds at what the writing plan set out to do—deliver an uncertain, uncomfortable ending without false resolution—while stumbling in a few notable places.

**Strengths:**

The Kellen scene is the chapter's strongest passage. The dialogue is restrained and effective: "Just wanted you to know what it cost" lands with real weight because it's not melodramatic. Kellen's accounting of the nephew's medicine costs is specific and grounded, which is exactly the kind of practical consequence the plan called for. The exchange avoids emotional declaration while still conveying genuine damage. "Try not to blow anything else up" as an exit line is perfectly calibrated—gallows humor without deflating the scene's gravity.

The creature's interaction with Tav in the cargo bay is mostly handled well. The physical detail of the creature lying in frozen coolant, the emergency blanket, the claw resting on Tav's boot—these are concrete and earned. The creature's behavior remains ambiguous; it doesn't "bond Disney-style," it just... doesn't pull away. That's the right register.

The galley meeting scene works structurally. Tav raising the Kade option creates genuine tension, and Reese's refusal is consistent with her character—she states her line without moralizing. Kellen's "stupidest thing I've ever done" is a good note to end that scene on.

**Weaknesses:**

The final section collapses into exactly the kind of sentimentality the plan explicitly warned against. "Three humans who'd chosen the harder path, and one creature that might be the last of its kind. No one knew what came next. But they'd find out together." This is the "found family warmth" the plan said to avoid. "They'd find out together" is a warm, reassuring ending that contradicts the stated intention of leaving things "uncertain, uncomfortable." The plan specifically said: "End without clear resolution—they made a choice, now they live in aftermath." The chapter ends with something closer to a hopeful ensemble moment.

Tav's monologue to the creature about their gambling problem and family is slightly too on-the-nose. The plan called for subtext and implication; having Tav explicitly narrate their backstory and motivations ("I wanted to be good at something. Wanted to matter. Thought maybe helping you—") is more tell than show. It's not terrible, but it's the weakest version of what could have been a more oblique scene.

The message draft scene ("Dear Mom and Mama...") is competent but predictable. The beat of writing and deleting and rewriting is a familiar emotional shorthand. It's not badly executed, but it's the most conventional moment in the chapter.

The line "Reese sat alone in the bridge and tried not to calculate all the ways she'd destroyed her crew's lives" is a mild tell-don't-show violation—we're told what she's doing internally rather than shown her doing something that implies it.

The chapter follows the plan reasonably well in terms of damage report, Kellen's accounting of costs, the crew meeting, and the uncertain ending—but it softens the ending considerably from what was intended. The plan called for "ending is uncertain, uncomfortable" and "not everyone agrees with the final choice; someone might leave." Nobody leaves, and the final image is warmer than planned.

**Dialogue assessment:** Generally strong. Kellen's lines are the best. Tav's galley dialogue is slightly expository. Reese's "That's the line" is good. The creature-adjacent dialogue (Tav talking to it) walks the line between character-revealing and slightly precious.

**Prose quality:** Clean and functional throughout. No purple prose. Sentence variation is appropriate—fragments in tense moments, longer constructions in quieter ones. The writing doesn't overreach, which is both a strength (no embarrassing failures) and a mild weakness (few moments of genuine distinction).

**Overall:** A competent, mostly faithful chapter that executes its quieter scenes well but softens its ending in a way that partially undermines the story's stated intentions. The Kellen scene is genuinely good. The final paragraph is the chapter's biggest failure.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a competent, readable space western that executes its premise with reasonable craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The world-building is economical and effective. Station 47, the corporate shell companies, the Orion Xenotech backstory, the war that glassed Hadrian-9—these details accumulate naturally without info-dumping. The setting feels lived-in. The "Dusty Comet" as a struggling independent hauler is a well-worn archetype but deployed with enough specificity (the broken environmental controls, the fuel cell Kellen has been nursing) to feel grounded.

Character differentiation is solid. Reese, Kellen, and Tav occupy distinct emotional registers: the haunted pragmatist, the practical loyalist with family obligations, and the idealistic dropout. Their conflict over the creature is the story's moral engine and it functions reasonably well. Kellen's nephew and the medication costs give the "hand it over" argument genuine weight rather than making it a straw man.

The dialogue is generally functional and occasionally sharp. "I just hate those bastards more than I like money" is a good line. The Kade confrontation has real tension. The banter between crew members feels natural.

WEAKNESSES:

The creature-as-last-of-its-kind is a well-worn science fiction trope, and the story doesn't subvert it in any meaningful way. The emotional beats—Tav bonding with the alien, Reese's Orion guilt, the crew choosing conscience over money—are all predictable from early chapters. The story follows the genre template closely without surprising the reader.

Tav's bonding with the creature in Chapter 5 is the most problematic section. The transition from "terrified of the alien" to "sitting companionably with it touching my boot" happens too quickly and too smoothly. The creature's behavior—methodically showing Tav the restraint buckle, cooperating with its own release—is convenient rather than earned. The "emotion without words" moment ("Not telepathy, nothing that clean") is a bit of a cheat that allows the author to have it both ways.

Reese's backstory (Jericho-7, fifteen years with Orion, the moment she stopped telling herself it was legal) is gestured at repeatedly but never shown. We're told she has a haunted past; we don't experience it. The Kade confrontation in Chapter 7 is meant to crystallize this backstory but instead just summarizes it through dialogue.

The prose is competent but rarely rises above functional. There are occasional strong images ("the air smelled like recycled atmosphere and someone frying synthetic protein") but also some flat passages. The ending is deliberately open-ended but feels more like an incomplete story than a satisfying conclusion—"no one knew what came next" is a weak final note.

The pacing in the middle chapters (4-6) sags. The hyperspace interlude, while character-building, slows momentum significantly. The creature eating power couplings is a nice detail but the scene with Tav and the deck panel feels padded.

The "last of its kind" revelation and the Hadrian-9 backstory are introduced and then not fully integrated into the story's emotional resolution. The moral weight of "we glassed their planet" is acknowledged but not really reckoned with.

The Kade character is introduced late and functions primarily as an exposition delivery device and antagonist-of-convenience. The "old colleague who stayed when I left" dynamic is underdeveloped.

Purple prose is largely absent—the writing is restrained, sometimes to a fault. Forced metaphors are rare. The piece is more guilty of being workmanlike than overwrought.

The transformation of Kellen from "hand it over" to "we're in it, so we finish it" is somewhat abrupt, though the line "Didn't say I wanted to give up" is a decent character moment.

Overall: a competent, readable genre piece that executes its premise without embarrassing itself but also without distinguishing itself. It's the kind of story that would be satisfying in a genre magazine but wouldn't linger in memory.
